1: Come down and sit in the dust, O virgin daughter of Babylon. The fall of Babylon is described through posture: from throne to ground-sitting. The virgin daughter imagery implies pride of untouched power -- Babylon has never been conquered. Sit on the ground without a throne strips the political symbol of authority: the throne gone, the ground is all that is left. The MKT preserves the address-form (O virgin daughter of Babylon) that treats the city as a person, allowing the shame-language to carry its full weight.
2: The imagery of captive women grinding at the mill and wading through streams -- manual labor and exposed vulnerability. The veil removed and the robe lifted expose the formerly veiled and robed queen. The MKT's specific clothing-items (veil, robe) follow the Hebrew's enumeration of the stripped dignities. Each item removed is a dignity taken: the veil (identity-privacy), the robe (status-display), the shod feet (social protection).
3: Your nakedness shall be exposed, your disgrace visible -- YHWH will take vengeance and will not relent (lo efga adam: I will not meet a man / not be dissuaded by anyone). The divine refusal to be negotiated out of judgment against Babylon is the counterpart to the divine compassion that could be interceded with for Israel. For Babylon, the cup of judgment is full and no mediator will succeed in emptying it.
4: YHWH is flagged -- the parenthetical identification of Israel's Redeemer: Our Redeemer -- the LORD of hosts is his name -- the Holy One of Israel. The bracket-verse inserts Israel's covenant title for YHWH into the oracle against Babylon. The Redeemer who is judging Babylon is specifically Israel's covenant God. The divine name YHWH of hosts (the army commander) identifies the one bringing Babylon low as the same one who called himself Israel's Kinsman-Redeemer.
5: Sit in silence and go into darkness, O daughter of the Chaldeans. The reversal of Babylon's previous position: formerly the mistress of kingdoms, now reduced to darkness and silence. The word mistress of kingdoms (geveret mamlakhot) is the highest political title -- over all kingdoms, not just one. The silence and darkness contrast with the Babylonian court's noise and splendor. The MKT preserves the title of greatness precisely because its loss is the point.
6: YHWH's self-disclosure of why he used Babylon: I was angry with my people, desecrated my heritage, handed them over to you. But Babylon showed no mercy -- she went beyond the instrument-role. On the elderly she laid an extremely heavy yoke. The instrument of divine discipline overstepped its mandate: using the people for plunder rather than correction. This excess of cruelty beyond the divine assignment is what turns the instrument into the target.
7: Olam (ancient time, disp 3) is flagged -- Babylon said I will be mistress forever. The claim to eternal power is the pride that precedes the fall. Le-olam here means permanently, forever -- Babylon claimed a permanence that belongs only to the LORD. The failure to consider how it would end (lo samti al-libbakh ahrit: not set on your heart its end/outcome) is the specific failure: pride so complete that the future is not contemplated.
8: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- who says in your heart, I am, and there is no one else beside me. The formula ani ve-afsi od (I am, and there is nothing more/no one else) is the usurpation of the divine self-description. In 45:6 and 45:18 this formula belongs to YHWH alone; here Babylon appropriates it. The widow and loss-of-children will not come -- Babylon's arrogant self-assurance is that her security is impervious. The yada is rendered who says -- the internal declaration of false omnipotence.
9: Both losses will come in a moment, in a single day: loss of children and widowhood. The completeness of the disaster is matched by its suddenness -- a single day. Despite the great power of Babylon's enchantments and sorceries, these losses will be total. The sorcery that Babylon has practiced from youth -- the whole apparatus of Babylonian astrology and divination -- will not prevent the sudden catastrophe.
10: You trusted in your wickedness -- the trust-vocabulary (batach) applied to evil as if it were a reliable foundation. No one sees me -- the denial of divine oversight. Your wisdom and knowledge led you astray (heshi'atekha: seduced/misled you) -- the Babylonian epistemic tradition (wisdom, knowledge, learning) became the agent of self-delusion. The ani ve-afsi od formula appears again: the knowledge that should have revealed YHWH instead reinforced self-sufficiency.
11: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged twice -- evil will come you will not know how to conjure away, disaster you will not be able to avert. The two yada-negatives: you will not know the charm (the correct ritual for averting); you will not be able to atone for it (kapper: cover/atone -- the sacrificial vocabulary of dealing with wrong). The whole Babylonian system of ritual knowledge for averting evil is useless against the divine decree.
12: The ironic invitation: stand by your enchantments and sorceries. The invitation is sarcastic -- perhaps you will succeed, perhaps you will inspire terror. The MKT's perhaps you will succeed; perhaps you will inspire terror preserves the ironic optative: maybe -- but both speaker and listener know it is futile. The catalogue of Babylonian occult expertise (enchantments, sorceries from youth) is precisely what will be shown to be nothing.
13: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- the advisers who divide the sky, gaze at the stars, give forecasts month by month are the astrologers. Let them stand up and save you -- the challenge to the astrologers to do what they claim. The MKT preserves the technical astronomical vocabulary: sky-dividers, star-gazers, monthly forecasters. Babylonian astrology was technically sophisticated; Isaiah is not dismissing the observation but the claim that such observation gives power.
14: Nefesh (soul, disp 3) is flagged -- there is no warm fire (gahalah) to sit beside it (nefesh: no coal for warming). The astrologers burned up like straw -- they cannot even save themselves from the flame. No warm ember for warming oneself. The MKT notes the nefesh here as not a flame warm enough to sit before. The fire that consumes the astrologers gives no warmth: a fire of pure destruction, not a fire of comfort.
15: Such are those you have dealt with -- your trading partners since your youth who now scatter, each in his own direction, with no one to save you. The commercial network (sochrekhah: your merchants) dissolves at the moment of crisis: each one goes off in his own direction. The social and commercial structure that depended on Babylon's power disintegrates exactly when it is most needed. The MKT's trading partners preserves the commercial vocabulary.
